# Meeting the ongoing challenge of avian influenza in the UK

Meeting the ongoing challenge of avian influenza in the UKGordon Hickman, head of the Department for Environment, Food & Rural Affairs’ (Defra) exotic disease control division, said that while the 2025/6 season had seen substantial losses, the decision to put in housing measures much earlier than in previous years seems to have successful.

Photo: SuppliedThe UK has suffered its third worst outbreak of highly pathogenic avian influenza (HPAI) with 100 cases reported during the 2025/6 season.

England has borne the brunt of the cases, recording 79 H5N1 HPAI, with 9 cases found in Scotland, 7 in Wales and 5 in Northern Ireland.

A further case of low pathogenic H5N1 was detected through zonal surveillance.

Almost 4 million birds lost More than 3.8 million birds have been culled or died as a result of the outbreaks, with chickens and turkeys bearing the brunt.

Although the numbers are low, 40% of the duck breeder population was affected by bird flu in the last season and 7% of turkeys.

Around 73% of cases have been large commercial farms with more than 1,000 birds with the remaining 27% found in backyard flocks.

East Anglia has been particularly badly hit due to the density of poultry.

--------------, head of the Department for Environment, Food & Rural Affairs’ (Defra) exotic disease control division, said that while the 2025/6 season had seen substantial losses, the decision to put in housing measures much earlier than in previous years seems to have successful.

Waterfowl the biggest culprit ------- said waterfowl continued to be the main reservoir of avian influenza.

Of the 930 birds that tested positive, 914 were found to have the H5N1 strain across 91 counties and 48 species.

Speaking to delegates at the British Pig and Poultry Fair, ------- urged producers to report dead wild birds, especially in East Anglia, stressing there would be comeback on the farmer if they tested positive.

He added the avian influenza virus continued to mutate and reassort, with the virus completely different from 2020/21.

Recent outbreaks of highly pathogenic H5NX (mainly H5N1) clade 2.3.4.4b have been Europe, Asia, Americas and Antarctica, with only Australasia relatively unaffected.

------- touched on the spillover of the virus into livestock in the US but said there was no evidence this had got into the UK’s dairy herd following substantial bulk milk testing.

But there continue to be cases in other mammals, including otters, seals, rats and foxes in parts of the world.

Biosecurity, biosecurity, biosecurity Biosecurity continued to be key to stopping the virus from spreading and -- urged producers to remain vigilant to ensure that wild birds and rats couldn’t spread it on farm.

Results of the UK’s vaccination programme in turkeys would be published later this summer although -- stressed the vaccine would not be available this year: “We have learned a lot from the trial… but it is important to note that vaccination is not a substitute for poor biosecurity,” -- added.

Trade barriers remain an issue, especially in Third Countries.

# EUMETNET celebrates 30 years of European meteorological services working together

EUMETNET celebrates 30 years of European meteorological services working together

Since its establishment in 1996, the organization has enabled its members to work together in key priority areas – from strengthening observation networks and improving the quality and accessibility of data to providing quality weather and climate services and fostering innovation.

The anniversary confirms the enduring value of European cooperation, which strengthens national capacities and brings tangible benefits to members and society.

EUMETNET was established with the aim of strengthening cooperation and representing the common interests of European national meteorological services.

Over time, however, it has evolved in line with advances in meteorological science and changing operational and political demands.

It has grown from a voluntary network into a mature and trustworthy partnership based on a long-term commitment to common goals and mutual trust.

Supporting members through joint activities
Over the past three decades, EUMETNET has enabled members to cooperate in a wide range of areas, thereby contributing to greater efficiency, coherence and innovation in meteorological science in Europe.

Through joint activities, members pool expertise, reduce duplication of work and take advantage of jointly developed solutions.

Such a model of cooperation enables national meteorological services to carry out their public tasks effectively, while strengthening the common European capacity to respond to changing user needs, technological developments and environmental challenges.

Value Beyond Borders
Since its creation, EUMETNET has gradually evolved from an initial focus on coordination to a comprehensive framework supporting a wide range of joint activities of the European meteorological services.

Early cooperation focused on improving the coverage of observations in low-data and remote areas, such as the North Atlantic and the Arctic, through radiosonde measurements from ships and observations from aircraft.

In parallel, the exchange of measurements from ground stations and radars has been launched, which has contributed to improving the quality and availability of data.

This initial work led to the establishment of the EUMETNET Composite Observing System (EUCOS), which represents an important milestone in European meteorology.

It has been shown that cooperation at European level can provide better spatial coverage of measurements, better quality data, unified European products based on national data and, ultimately, more accurate weather forecasts at lower costs.

Building on these successes, observation capabilities have been gradually upgraded with additional methods, including observations at sea, measurements of atmospheric conditions at different altitudes – such as wind, temperature, humidity and airborne particles – and, more recently, pollen monitoring.

At the same time, cooperation from observation has been extended to weather forecasting, climate services, aviation meteorology, education and training, data policy and data accessibility, as well as joint representation of interests before key European and global organisations.

In the field of forecasting, one of the most prominent achievements of EUMETNET is the MeteoAlarm system.

It is an early warning system for weather hazards that combines, coordinates, displays and transmits weather warnings issued by the European national meteorological services.

In addition to the growing need for data and rapid technological developments, EUMETNET has also developed a data infrastructure for data exchange, MeteoGate, which enables easier access to meteorological data for members and external users.

In parallel, EUMETNET has launched a number of initiatives to use artificial intelligence and machine learning in weather, climate and environmental applications.

These achievements show how, over three decades, EUMETNET has developed into an organisation that brings together European capabilities in the fields of observation, forecasting, climate services, aviation, data sharing and innovation.
Members' contributions
EUMETNET's collective achievements are the result of the dedication and diverse contributions of its members, who are involved in the work of the organisation in different but equally important ways.

Some take responsibility for implementing joint programs on behalf of all members, while others contribute their data, knowledge and expertise for the benefit of the wider community.

The Environment Agency became a member of EUMETNET on 1 January 2008.

The accession agreement was signed by Dr Klemen Bergant, then Director of the Office of Meteorology and Hydrology at the Environment Agency, who has been the Executive Director of EUMETNET since 2021.

Within the organisation, the Environment Agency is involved in joint cooperation programmes in the fields of observation, forecasting, climate services, aeronautical meteorology and research and development.

The most important programs include OPERA (International Radar Data Exchange and Radar Composite Preparation), eGAFOR (Provision of Cross-Border Aeronautical Forecasting) and MeteoA

# European Recovery and Resilience Funds – Together towards the goal: sustainable cultural heritage

European Recovery and Resilience Funds – Together towards the goal: sustainable cultural heritage

There are currently 31,095 heritage units registered in the Register of Immovable Cultural Heritage, of which 8,484 units have been declared a cultural monument of national or local importance.

The preserved cultural heritage significantly contributes to the visibility of the country and thus to the development of tourism, which was one of the most affected economic sectors during the COVID-19 pandemic.

The state and local communities are also striving to preserve the rich cultural heritage with the help of European funds.

As part of the Recovery and Resilience Plan (RRP), the Ministry of Culture, together with the municipalities, is implementing an investment for the sustainable restoration and revival of cultural heritage.

Sustainable restoration and revitalisation of cultural heritage and public cultural infrastructure – investment
The objective of the investment is to support the sustainable development of tourism through the revival of cultural heritage and public cultural infrastructure.

The investment shall include the renovation, renovation, integrated revitalisation and modernisation of public cultural heritage facilities.
€42 million from the Recovery and Resilience Facility (the Facility) is foreseen for the implementation of the investment.

# Dutch government moves towards mandatory avian influenza vaccination

Dutch government moves towards mandatory avian influenza vaccinationThe government is working together with the poultry sector on a plan to make vaccination against avian influenza mandatory for laying hen farms.

Image created with the help of AI.The Dutch government is working on a plan to make avian influenza vaccination compulsory for all laying hen farms in the country.

Trade barriers remain a major concern.

The government is working together with the poultry sector on a plan to make vaccination against avian influenza mandatory for laying hen farms.

This year, 45 poultry farms have been infected with bird flu.

As a result, almost 2.3 million animals have been culled to prevent the spread of the virus.

“Avian influenza can be highly disruptive for animals, businesses and society,” said the responsible State Secretary for Agriculture, -------------.

“Together with the poultry sector, we are therefore taking a new step towards tackling the virus more effectively.

By focusing on measures such as vaccination, we can begin to reduce avian influenza in a structural way.

In doing so, we are pursuing an internationally accepted approach so that it does not come at the expense of our trade.”No

trade barriersWork on vaccination against the virus has been ongoing for some time.

A field trial has shown that vaccinated animals significantly reduce the risk of a large-scale avian influenza outbreak.

Infections can also still be detected quickly through the mandatory European monitoring programme.

In addition, the first vaccination pilot is progressing well.

“The table eggs from vaccinated hens have been sold on the Dutch market and, so far, no trade barriers have arisen,” according to the Ministry of Agriculture.

In a letter to Parliament, ------ wrote that trading partners are highly appreciative of the transparent way in which the Netherlands has organised and communicated the entire pilot programme.

“The poultry sector has made a constructive contribution to this,” ------ stated.

The ----- will continue until the end of December this year.

Trade barriers remain a major concern regarding the use of vaccination.

For that reason, vaccination is being introduced step by step.

International cooperation is underway to secure acceptance of products from vaccinated poultry.

The Netherlands has taken the initiative to establish an international working group with the US, Canada, France, the UK, the European Commission and the World Organisation for Animal Health.

Plan for mandatory vaccination ------ will spend the coming months working with the poultry sector on an action plan for mandatory vaccination of laying hens against avian influenza.

The action plan is expected to be completed by the end of this year.

However, the poultry sector has set a number of conditions for introducing a vaccination programme.

These are focused on the international acceptance of products from vaccinated poultry.

In addition, the poultry sector is calling for a feasible and affordable surveillance programme.

“The export value of products such as table eggs amounts to around €700 million.

It is therefore of great importance that trade in poultry and poultry products is not harmed.

A careful approach and a proper assessment of the impact on the sector are essential,” ------ wrote in his letter to Parliament.

Flu jab for poultry farmersAlthough the risk of avian influenza transmission to humans is still considered very low, the government is nevertheless taking precautionary measures.

People considered most at risk of infection – including poultry farmers, ------------- and animal ambulance staff – will be offered the opportunity to receive the seasonal influenza vaccination, commonly known as the flu jab, from the next flu season onwards.

This vaccination is not directly intended to reduce the likelihood of people becoming infected with avian influenza.

The primary aim is to prevent any bird flu viruses they may encounter from mixing with human influenza strains.

Pig farmers who may come into contact with swine flu viruses will also be offered the flu jab in order to prevent animal influenza viruses from combining with human strains.

According to public health experts, vaccinating people specifically against bird flu currently offers little added value.

Fewer farms in high-risk areasThe intensified action plan for tackling avian influenza and reducing the risks of zoonotic diseases also examined structural measures to reduce the number of poultry farms in poultry-dense and water-rich areas.

An impact assessment is currently underway into the consequences for public and animal health, as well as for the poultry sector.

The results are expected before the summer.

“These are far-reaching measures that require a solid evidence base and new legal powers.

The guiding principle is that the measures must be appropriate, necessary and proportionate,” ------ said.

# Pune’s Government Veterinary Institute Develops Goatpox Vaccine, Set to Reach Farmers Soon

Pune’s Government Veterinary Institute Develops Goatpox Vaccine, Set to Reach Farmers

Soon (ETV Bharat)Pune:

The Veterinary Biological Products Institute in Aundh, Pune, has developed a vaccine for goatpox.

It has become the first government institution in India to receive a licence for producing it.

The vaccine is expected to be supplied to livestock owners across the state in the coming weeks.

Goatpox is a contagious disease affecting goats and sheep.

Until now, the state government relied on private companies to procure more than one crore vaccine doses annually for livestock vaccination.

Officials said the Pune-based institute has been working on the goatpox vaccine for the past four years.

The vaccine has now been successfully developed and is awaiting final clearance from the Centre.

Authorities expect it to be administered to animals before the monsoon.

--------------------, Joint Commissioner at the Veterinary Biological Products Manufacturing Institute, said the state had earlier depended on private companies for vaccines against both goatpox and lumpy skin disease.

The government relied on external suppliers while many animals continued to be affected by the diseases.

-- said the institute acquired vaccine technology from the Indian Veterinary Research Institute, after which scientists in Pune underwent specialised training before developing the vaccine.

After preparing experimental batches, three samples were sent to the Biological Standardisation Division in Bareilly for quality testing.

Field trials were later conducted at five government farms in Maharashtra, where the vaccine was administered to around 500 cattle and 100 buffaloes.

The trials were monitored by the National Institute of Virology and central government teams.

Following successful testing, the institute received a commercial production licence from the Central Drugs Standard Control Organisation and the local licensing authority.

The final report from Bareilly is expected by May 30.Dr.

Pathan said distribution of the vaccine directly to farmers is expected to begin in the first week of June 2026.He added that Maharashtra requires nearly 1.15 crore doses of goatpox vaccine every year, for which the government spends over Rs 5 crore through private procurement.

The newly developed vaccine will reduce production costs.

“Private vaccines cost around Rs 20 to Rs 30 per dose, while our vaccine can be produced for just Rs 6 to Rs 7 per dose, which will help the state save substantially on vaccination expenses,” he said.

So far, the institute has successfully produced 13 to 14 batches of the goatpox vaccine with nearly 50 to 55 lakh doses.

The institute is also working on a vaccine for lumpy skin disease.

Officials said testing has been completed and the licensing process is in its final stage.

The lumpy vaccine is expected to receive approval within the next two to three months and could be made available for livestock in Maharashtra by November or December 2026.Read

# New episode of the podcast Mission: Quality: Sustainability as a Path to Quality

New episode of the podcast Mission:

Quality: Sustainability as a Path to Quality

Author: Ministry of Education
At the forefront of the conversation is the understanding of sustainability as a long-term and thoughtful process that goes beyond individual projects or activities.

Sustainability at school is not just about ecological practices, but a way of thinking, collaborating, and collaboratively creating changes that remain part of the daily life of the school community.

The guests will present how they build a culture of quality at the school through systematic monitoring of work, common goals and the development of teachers' and students' competencies.

The conversation raises the question of how to anchor the changes so that they become permanent, meaningful and related to the real needs of the school.

Special emphasis is also placed on ecological sustainability, which is lived at the school through everyday practices, involving students in sustainable activities and promoting a responsible attitude towards the environment.

It is the small but consistent steps that show how sustainability can become a natural part of school life.

We also touch on the importance of relationships, cooperation and a shared vision.

Sustainability is not only a question of content, but also of the way people in school collaborate, listen to each other and develop a quality learning environment together.

You are invited to listen to the new episode of the podcast Mission: Quality if you are interested in how sustainability, both pedagogical, organizational and ecological, can become a driver of quality development in education.

# Minister Vlajčić at the AGRIFISH Council: Food security must remain a strategic priority of the European Union

Minister Vlajčić at the AGRIFISH Council: Food security must remain a strategic priority of the European Union

Photo Gallery
On 26 May 2026, Deputy Prime Minister and Minister of Agriculture, Forestry and Fisheries David Vlajčić participated in a meeting of the Council of Ministers of Agriculture and Fisheries (AGRIFISH) in Brussels, where the main topics were the availability and price of fertilisers, trade-related agricultural issues and the resilience of European agriculture in the current geopolitical circumstances.

Speaking about the rise in fertilizer prices and market disturbances, the Minister pointed out that the increased input costs directly affect the sustainability of agricultural production and food prices for citizens.

"Our farmers cannot bear the burden of disruptions in the world market and rising energy and fertilizer prices alone.

That is why crisis measures are important, but it is equally important to ensure greater European self-sufficiency and stability of production", said Minister Vlajčić.

He welcomed the European Commission's Fertilizer Action Plan and stressed that food security must remain one of the key elements of the European Union's strategic autonomy.

In the discussion, he pointed out that geopolitical instability, the consequences of sanctions against Russia and Belarus, the rise in energy prices, the CBAM mechanism and the escalation of the conflict in the Middle East have further increased the pressure on European food production and the fertilizer market.

In doing so, he stressed the need for a fast, operationally simple and targeted implementation of aid measures in order to preserve the competitiveness of European agriculture.

In particular, it supported the mobilisation of the EU's agricultural reserve and the possibility of additional liquidity support measures within the existing national CAP envelopes.

He also emphasized the importance of long-term reduction of dependence on classic mineral fertilizers and external disturbances through the development of circular and sustainable solutions.

"We will support all solutions that reduce dependence on classic mineral fertilizers and external disturbances.

This means more organic and biologically based fertilizers, better use of biomass, biogas and biomethane, and smarter use of the resources we already have," Vlajčić said.

Speaking about agricultural issues related to trade, Vlajčić warned that the current geopolitical instability is increasingly affecting global supply chains, energy prices, transport routes and the total cost of food production.

In particular, he emphasized that the war in Ukraine, instability in the Middle East, trade tensions between major global economies and the increasing fragmentation of international trade are putting additional pressure on European farmers and the food sector.

He supported the continued strengthening of the European Union's network of trade agreements, but with a clear need to protect European production and preserve a level playing field.

He stressed that any further market opening must be accompanied by detailed impact assessments and effective and swiftly applicable safeguards for sensitive agricultural sectors.

The European Union's trade policy must remain aligned with the objectives of food security, production resilience and the preservation of high European standards of food quality, safety and sustainability.

Of the miscellaneous items, Minister Vlajčić emphasized the importance of labelling the country of origin of food at the level of the European Union.

Croatia supports the extension of mandatory labelling of origin for food in order to increase transparency towards consumers and strengthen the competitiveness of European agriculture.

The issue of giant cormorants was also raised at the meeting, with Croatia supporting the Czech initiative to launch a European management plan for giant cormorants due to the damage this species causes to fishermen and breeders.

# Slovenia successfully issues a variable rate bond in the amount of EUR 350 million

Slovenia successfully issues a variable rate bond in the amount of EUR 350 million

Author: Ministry of Finance

Following the announcement by Slovenia on Tuesday, 26 May 2026 that it had granted a mandate to OTP banka and UniCredit Banka Slovenija to lead the issuance of a seven-year euro-denominated variable rate bond, the order book for the new issue opened today at approximately 8:45 a.m. with an initial price guidance of 20 basis points ahead of the six-month Euribor.

Demand was strong from the start, reaching €375 million at 11 a.m., allowing the originally announced release size to increase from €250 million to €350 million.

Almost the entire share of subscribers was represented by investors from Slovenia (96 percent), and the rest by investors from Austria.

Banks dominated by the type of investor (90 percent), while insurance companies (six percent) and investment funds (four percent) also subscribed for bonds to a lesser extent.

With this issue, the State Treasury responded primarily to the interest of Slovenian banks, which expressed interest in government securities with variable interest rates.

At the same time, this type of transaction complements the range of financing instruments and contributes to the further diversification of Slovenia's investor base.

# Regional cooperation for safe, transparent and digital real estate systems

Regional cooperation for safe, transparent and digital real estate systems

On 26 May 2026, a regional round table entitled "From Property to Progress: Digital Transformation and EU Integration of the Western Balkans" was held in Tirana.

The event brought together representatives of cadastral and geodetic administrations from the Western Balkans and neighbouring European Union Member States.

The central topic of the meeting was strengthening cooperation in ensuring safe and transparent property rights, digitization of real estate services and the development of efficient land administration.

The round table was organised as part of the joint action of the European Union and the Council of Europe »
Improving the protection of the right to property and facilitating the enforcement of judgments of the European Court of Human Rights in Albania (D-REX III)

[KM1]
"Improving the protection of the right to property and facilitating execution of ECtHR judgments in Albania" or D-REX III (D-REX III is the title of the third phase of the joint action of the European Union and the Council of Europe in Albania, which aims to improve the protection of property rights and more effective enforcement of judgments of the European Court of Human Rights in Albania.

The project is implemented within the framework of the programme
Horizontal facility for the Western Balkans and Turkey (page in English).

[KM2]
(English: Horizontal Facility for the Western Balkans and Türkiye), in close partnership with the Albanian State Cadastral Agency).

The Geodetic Administration of the Republic of Slovenia was represented at the meeting by Director General Tomaž Petek, Director of the Real Estate Office mag.

Ema Pogorelčnik and Head of the Project Unit for European and Strategic Projects Matjaž Grilc.

The right to property as the foundation of trust
Regulated property rights are one of the foundations of legal certainty, economic development and trust in institutions.

Cadastral and real estate systems play a very concrete role in this.

They ensure that real estate data is correct, accessible, transparent and useful for citizens, businesses and public institutions.

At the roundtable, participants emphasized that digital transformation is not just a technical upgrade of existing processes.

It is also an opportunity for better services, faster procedures, greater transparency and better protection of users' rights.

In his opening remarks,
His Excellency Edi Rama, Prime Minister of the Republic of Albania
, underlined the importance of the event, underlined the strategic importance of digital transformation in property management and the valuable role of regional cooperation in advancing this agenda.

Lorena Goxhobelli, Director-General of the Albanian State Cadastral Agency,
highlighted the crucial need to advance collaboration among all stakeholders to ensure that each property is accurately registered and effectively managed.

Giulia Re, Head of the Council of Europe Office in Tirana
, stressed that "ensuring secure and transparent property rights is fundamental to upholding human dignity and the rule of law.

Regional cooperation and digital transformation are key to building stronger institutions that serve citizens, build trust and support sustainable development.

"
Andre Rizzo, Chargé d'affaires of the Delegation of the European Union to Albania,
reaffirmed the EU's commitment to advancing digitalisation and legal harmonisation among the beneficiaries of the Western Balkans and ensuring that every citizen enjoys the same property rights.

Digital transformation of real estate services
Representatives of the participating countries discussed the modernisation of cadastral systems, the digitalisation of services, the use of artificial intelligence and compliance with European human rights standards in the field of property rights.

Delegations from Albania, Greece, Kosovo*, Montenegro, North Macedonia, Romania, Serbia and Slovenia took part in the discussion.

*Note: The designation Kosovo is used without prejudice to positions on status and is in line with UN Security Council Resolution 1244 and the ICJ Opinion on the Kosovo declaration of independence.

In practice, the digitalisation of real estate services means easier access to data, fewer administrative barriers and more transparent procedures.

For users, this means faster service and greater predictability.

For institutions, it means better data management, better quality of records and more efficient decision-making.

The discussion showed that countries in the region are facing similar challenges.

These include updating records, linking different data sources, ensuring data quality, building user trust and adapting to European standards.

Regional cooperation as a path to joint solutions
The event was designed as an open platform for the exchange of reform experiences and the search for practical solutions for more transparent, efficient and user-friendly real estate services.

Such meetings are important because they allow for the comparison of different practices and the transfer of knowledge between countries.

Real Estate System

# Measures to prevent the spread of the Japanese beetle

Measures to prevent the spread of the Japanese beetle

Pheromone trap |
By: Food Safety, Veterinary and Plant Protection Administration
1 / 4
Pheromone trap
Japanese beetle
Eaten leaves
The Japanese beetle was first found in Slovenia in the summer of 2024 at the motorway rest areas of Lukovica and Barje (Ljubljana), where it was most likely carried by a vehicle from northern Italy.

In 2025, the number of finds increased.

Because the Japanese beetle
included in the EU list of quarantine harmful organisms of plants, the prescribed measures must be implemented when it is found to prevent the further spread of
Yes.

Installing traps
Since mid-May, UVHVVR in cooperation with the Agricultural Institute of Slovenia (KIS) has been installing special pheromone traps for monitoring adult beetles at selected locations in the municipalities of Ljubljana, Brezovica, Dobrova-Polhov Gradec, Ig, Lukovica, Dol pri Ljubljani, Domžale, Kamnik and Moravče.

They are used to determine whether the pest is present in a certain area and whether it also occurs outside the already known infested areas in Barje (Ljubljana, location of the AC rest area Barje - south) and in Lukovica.

Traps contain small amounts of attracting substances (pheromones and plant attractants) that are not dangerous to humans or pets.

UVHVVR and KIS ask citizens not to touch, open or move traps.

Any intervention can interfere with the results of monitoring and complicate the timely detection and eradication of the Japanese beetle.

Why is the Japanese beetle harmful?

The Japanese beetle is native to Northeast Asia and has been present in Europe since 2014.

It is widespread in Piedmont and Lombardy in Italy, the canton of Ticino in Switzerland and parts of Germany, and from there it is regularly transported as a stowaway to other areas via trucks, cars or trains.

When the vehicle stops, the beetles fly out to find host plants nearby.

It attacks more than 300 species of cultivated and wild plants.

The beetles eat the leaves, which acquire a lacy appearance, fruits and flowers, and the larvae damage the roots of the grass cress.

He prefers vines, stone fruits, apple trees, hazelnuts, roses, vines, berries, actinidia, beans, soybeans, maple, elm, linden, horse chestnut and many other plants.

Since it multiplies rapidly and has no natural enemies, it can cause extensive damage in the urban environment, in gardens and agricultural areas.

How do we recognize it?

Adult beetles are about one centimeter in size, oval, metallic green with copper-brown caps.

They have characteristic white tufts of hair on the side and back.

The Japanese beetle is often confused with the garden beetle, which has hairy caps and is devoid of white tufts of hairs.

Citizens should be on the lookout for the possible presence of the Japanese beetle and characteristic plant damage.

In case of suspicion, they should immediately inform the UVHVVR (
gp.uvhvvr@gov.si
) or CIS (
info@kis.si
).

They should attach a photo, exact location, date of finding and contact information.

Early detection and reporting allows for faster action by professional services and reduces economic damage.

# An engineering spark to young innovators, an artificial intelligence project and a mentor that inspires young people

An engineering spark to young innovators, an artificial intelligence project and a mentor that inspires young people

Engineering Spark 2026 Awards and Recipients.

|
Author: Andrej Križ
Engineering Spark Awards are received annually
groundbreaking, original practices, projects, events or persons who have made a significant contribution to the development, validity and enthusiasm for the engineering profession and innovation in society.

Among the awardees are often individuals, projects and organizations from the school environment, which has a significant impact on the definition of young people to enter the engineering world.

This year's selection was attended by representatives of the Ministry of Higher Education, Science and Innovation, the Chamber of Electrical Industry of Slovenia, the Office for Knowledge Transfer of the University of Ljubljana, Spirit Slovenia, the Engineering Academy of Slovenia and the ambassadors of the project Engineers and Engineers We Will!.

At a time when Europe is investing rapidly in technological development, artificial intelligence, digitalisation and the green transition, young people's enthusiasm for STEM fields is becoming one of the key development priorities of society.

The Development Report 2025 of the Office of the Republic of Slovenia for Macroeconomic Analysis and Development (UMAR) warns that the creativity of young people in Slovenia remains among the lowest in the EU, despite the fact that creativity, innovation and interdisciplinary thinking are becoming the key competencies of the future society.

The mission of Engineering Spark is focused on just that - recognizing and encouraging individuals, teams and environments that awaken young people's curiosity, creativity and courage to develop new ideas and connect them with the real challenges of modern society.

Peter Volasko, MSc, from the Directorate for Science and Innovation of the Ministry of Higher Education, Science and Innovation, pointed out: "The future of Slovenia and Europe is inextricably linked to excellent knowledge, innovation and the promotion of natural sciences and technology.

The development of new technologies brings exceptional opportunities, but at the same time an increasing need for personnel, which are already in short supply.

That's why initiatives like the Engineering Spark are crucial – because they bring engineering closer to young people as a profession of the present and the future, and encourage them to develop their ideas into solutions with a real impact on society.

"
Engineering Spark Recipients 2026
Smart helmet Mark II
(project of pupils of the Primary School Vide Pregarc, Ljubljana)
Smart helmet Mark II
, a project of students of the Vida Pregarc Primary School from Ljubljana, represents a cutting-edge innovation by young artists that combines security, smart sensors and digital mapping in one product.

The project was created as part of the LegoRobotics interest activity, with which the students first presented themselves at the FIRST LEGO League competition, and then upgraded the idea and won an exceptional 1st place at the national POPRI competition.

The smart helmet is an innovative protective equipment for cavers and archaeologists, which enables automatic adjustment of lighting, detection of dangerous gases and simultaneous 3D modeling of underground spaces.

A smart helmet thus acts not only as protective equipment, but as a smart tool for safe and digitally supported research.

The fact that the students developed the idea up to two working prototypes and also patented their product, which confirms its originality and utility value, is particularly impressive.

The students Tobija Holcar and Svit Bobek, who were the driving force of the team in the development of the innovation project, contributed the most to the top achievement, and Katja Presetnik and Mitja Gracar provided important mentoring support.

The project is at the heart of the Engineering Spark, as it proves that young people with curiosity, creativity, teamwork and technical knowledge can develop innovations with real social significance and start on the path of engineering creation at a very early age.

Mentor Dominik Trstenjak:

From idea to innovation through the FIRST LEGO League
(
Elementary School Pod goro Slovenske Konjice);
Dominik Trstenjak, teacher and mentor in programs
FIRST LEGO League Challengers & Explorers
At the Pod goro Slovenske Konjice Elementary School, with her many years of dedicated work with young people, she has been making a decisive contribution to the development of creative, cooperative and socially responsible engineering thinking among children and adolescents.

Through the FIRST program, the LEGO League brings robotics and technology closer to young people.

What's more: it gives them a space in which they can build their confidence, curiosity and courage to explore complex challenges.

His mentorship is based on a belief in the potential of young people, encouraging independent thinking and understanding that the best ideas are often the result of cooperation, perseverance and a willingness to learn from mistakes.

The special value of Trstenjak's work is that young people under his mentorship develop solutions that go beyond school projects and respond to the real needs of society.

That's how it is
Aid for people with mobility impairments
developed by students under his mentorship.

It was successfully registered as a European model with the EU Intellectual Property Office (EUIPO), which is the first achievement of its kind in the history of the FIRST LEGO League in Slovenia and beyond.

# Before we judge, let's ask: the forest deserves knowledge and respect

Before we judge, let's ask: the forest deserves knowledge and respect

Walking Between Five Stations |
Author: Slovenian Forest Service
1 / 3
Head of the Department for Forest Measures of the Bled Forest Management District Lucija Odar
Three ponds
The Week of Forests is an annual campaign to promote forests and forestry, which takes place in the last week of May, and it traditionally hosts numerous events of forestry institutions throughout Slovenia.

This year's Forest Week is held under the motto "Less Mistakes, More Forest Knowledge and Respect", which communicates that the public has a significant impact on the future of forests, but that this influence should be made aware of and based on forest knowledge.

The central event was conceived at the ZGS as an invitation to a better public conversation about forests: with less quick judgments, fewer simplifications, more verified information and more trust in the profession.

At five stations, forestry experts from various ZGS regional units demonstrated that a young forest is not a void, but the next generation of forest; that felling is not a goal, but only one of the possible measures; that free access to forests also entails responsibility; that every major cut is not a barefoot; and that the city forest is not a park that is merely "arranged" for visitors, but a living forest ecosystem.

"We understand that certain interventions in the forest raise questions and concerns in people.

That is why we at ZGS can be your first point for clarification, as we are always available to remove any doubts.

The task of the forester is not only to decide, but also to explain: why a certain measure is necessary, what it means for the forest today and, above all, what it means for the forest in decades to come.

Only active and systematic forest management creates the forest we want in the future: healthy and resilient to climate change.

The forest deserves knowledge and respect.

And when we have questions about encroachment on the forest, we should first ask the profession," explained the director of the ZGS, Gregor Danev.
Special attention was paid to the work of the forester at the event.

In the forest, he observes, plans, advises owners, monitors diseases and pests, selects trees for the care of stands, directs restoration after damage, participates in fire protection, takes care of the professional basis of decisions and helps the public understand what is happening in the forest.

"A forester doesn't just come into the woods with a chainsaw.

It comes with knowledge, responsibility and a plan for the future of the forest," explained Lucija Odar, Head of the Forest Action Service of the Bled Regional Unit at one of the stations.

Alenka Korenjak, MSc, from the Ministry of Agriculture, Forestry and Food, emphasized that today's society sovereignly expresses its views on forests and is increasingly sensitive to the management of forests.

Therefore, the work of the public forestry service goes beyond the framework of professional forestry knowledge and requires knowledge and competences with which it can respond to the needs of the changing environment and society.

"Forestry experts in the field must be able to explain in a publicly understandable way the works carried out in the forest, their aim and purpose.

In addition, they must be able to communicate appropriately with the public, educate them and raise awareness of the importance of forests and forestry.

G
I congratulate my colleagues on the extremely important work you are doing
" she said.

The choice of the Three Ponds location was carefully considered, as it represents a transition between a landscaped city park, a recreational space and an actual city forest.

"Urban forests are of great importance for Maribor, as they significantly contribute to the quality of living, recreation and contact between people and nature.

Forest Week 2026 opens up an important dialogue between the profession, the local community and the public, and encourages more respect for the natural environment.

Maribor remains a city that is aware of the importance of green areas and urban forests for the well-being of its citizens," said Srečko Vilar, Deputy Mayor of the Municipality of Maribor, in his address.

It is in urban forests that communication with the public is crucial, said mag.

Alenka Korenjak.

These forests are
because of their roles
for residents of cities and larger settlements
of the utmost importance, which, according to her,
Municipality
Maribor is very aware, as it is already ten years ago
The first urban forests were declared special purpose forests.

In cooperation with the Slovenian Forest Service, it takes care of the precise implementation of forestry measures, designs recreational and educational infrastructure, maintains it and implements programmes with experts from various disciplines to educate and raise awareness of all generations of visitors to urban forests.

As pointed out at the ZGS, they remain available to the public for all questions about the forest, interventions, restoration, forest etiquette, urban forests and the work of the public forestry service.

"If there is anything in the woods that bothers you, worries you or interests you, ask.

Our foresters are in the field every day, among people and in the forest, and they are a treasure trove of knowledge.

We prefer to answer ten difficult questions rather than having one misconception about the forest become a common belief," added Igor Kopše, Head of the Regional Unit of the Maribor Fire Brigade.

# Reminder of Key Marketing and Welfare Requirements for Eggs and Laying Hens

Reminder of Key Marketing and Welfare Requirements for Eggs and Laying Hens

Date published: 
26 May 2026

 Following the recent lifting of avian influenza restrictions, DAERA has today provided guidance to the industry of its statutory obligations, particularly in relation to free range egg production.

EU Egg Marketing Regulations remain directly applicable under the Windsor Framework, and the Welfare of Farmed Animals Regulations continue to apply.

In particular, following the removal of the Veterinary Housing Order, producers must carefully consider the requirement for birds to have direct access to the range as they readjust to outdoor conditions.

“Direct” access to range requires that from the popholes hens can see and easily access an open-air run that is mainly covered with vegetation and provides appropriate overhead cover.

Care should be taken to avoid structures or surfaces that may hinder birds’ ability or willingness to access any part of the range.

Evidence of compliance includes birds exhibiting normal behaviour and actively accessing and dispersing throughout the range area.

DAERA recognises that, particularly where units have been converted to free range production, access to the required range area may be restricted by roads, lanes or waterways.

Where roads or lanes must be crossed, the construction of tunnels, underpasses or overpasses for the hens will be needed.

These should be carefully designed, taking account of height, width, drainage and light penetration, to encourage (rather than impede) the hens’ natural ranging behaviour.

Producers intending to commence table egg production or make changes to an existing egg laying establishment must apply to DAERA’s Egg Marketing Inspectorate at least one month in advance of placing hens or implementing any changes.

This ensures sufficient time for necessary process.

Further information.

Notes to editors:
Follow DAERA on X formerly called Twitter and Facebook.

All media queries should be directed to the DAERA Press Office: pressoffice.group@daera-ni.gov.uk or telephone: 028 9016 3460.The Executive Information Service operates an out of hours’ service for media enquiries only between 1800hrs and 0800hrs Monday to Friday and at weekends and public holidays.

The duty press officer can be contacted on 028 9037 8110.

# Why this unexplained case of avian flu is forcing epidemiologists to change their strategy (and the only effective solution)

Why this unexplained case of avian flu is forcing epidemiologists to change their strategy (and the only effective solution)

For decades, the rule of bird flu was simple: to get sick, you had to have direct contact with an infected bird or cattle. But a recent medical anomaly

n British Columbia has shattered this reassuring certainty. A ------- contracted the dreaded H5N1 virus with no kno

n animal exposure, raising the dreaded specter of human-to-human transmission. Faced with this evolving threat, mathematical modellers have just simulated ou

response capabilities. Their conclusions are clear: our usual emergency protocols are already obsolete.
The

conundrum of contactless contamination
Until now, the H5N1 virus was considered a strict

zoonotic threat. Public health experts agreed that the pathogen only jumped to humans in very specific environments, such as

oultry farms or dairy farms. Human-to-human transmission had never been formally established in a sustainable way.
However, the recent case identified in Canada escapes any epidemiological logic. The p

tient did not visit any farms or handle any wild birds. This complete lack of a "source" h

as forced ------------------------ and his team at York University to consider the w

rst-case scientific scenario: the virus may already be circulating quiet

y from one human being to another.
The race against time for viral mutations
The real danger of H5N1 lies not only in its lethality, but in its incredible adaptability. Influenza viruses are known to mutate at lightning speed. If it manages to adapt perfectly to the human body, this pathogen could trigger local chains of transmission with disastrous global repercussions.
The major anxiet

of researchers lies in the invisibility of the enemy. If human-to-huma

transmission is confirmed, it is highly likely that asymptomatic carriers are already circulating the virus. By the time the health system identifies a severe case with sy

mptoms, the chain of contamination has already silently branched out thro

ghout the community.
Credit: Public domainColorized transmission electron micrograph of avian influenza A H5N1 virus.
Reactive vacci

ation is already doomed to failure
To anticipate a potential pandemic, the modelling lab simulated an outbreak in the highly agricultural region of Abbotsford. Researchers have virtually tested the effectiveness of our best weapons: strict isolation and emergency vaccinati

on campaigns launched after the discovery of a first patient.
The results of these simulations are particularly cruel for our traditional health policies. The study, published in Nature

Health, shows that "reactive" vaccination provides almost no additional benefit compared to simple home isolation. By the time a vaccine is distributed and becomes effective, the virus has alre

ady won the race.
The preventive shield as the only salvation
Faced with this ruthless viral dynamic,

athematical models validate only one real containment strategy: preventive vaccination. To have any hope of breaking chains of transmission before they even form, it is impe

ative that the authorities vaccinate at-risk populations, such as farmers and their families

, ahead of any human crisis.
This evidence offers a clear warning to governments around the world. Waiting for a human cluster to appear before reacting will not be enough to contain H5N1. The immed

ate isolation of the sick will remain crucial, but only a massive vaccination anticipation among exposed professionals can prevent this avian virus from becoming the next great human pandemic.

fulltext:
# Giraffe deaths caused by lumpy skin disease

Giraffe deaths caused by lumpy skin disease

Giraffe mortalities may have been caused by lumpy skin disease (LSD), the Namibian Agricultural Union reported in their weekly newsletter dated 8 May 2026.

Last year, from mid-April to the end of May, Namibia experienced an unusual wave of giraffe mortalities across large parts of the country.

Farmers reported finding dead giraffes and observing sick animals displaying abnormal behaviour.

Reports came from areas west and north of Okahandja, extending to Omaruru and Waterberg, as well as farms around Outjo, Okaukuejo and Maroelaboom.

There were also rumours of deaths inside Etosha National Park.

Observed symptoms included drooping ears, excessive drooling, eye discharge and apparent blindness.

Some giraffes developed skin lesions resembling lumpy skin disease (LSD).

Farmers also described lethargic animals standing motionless in the bush and showing little reaction to approaching vehicles or people.

Others appeared disoriented, stumbling over bushes and fences with a staggered, “drunk-like” gait.

Unfortunately, too few samples and questionnaires were submitted to Wildlife Vets Namibia, who sought to determine the cause of mortalities, to determine the exact cause.

Several alternative causes were investigated and ruled out.

These included

Plant poisoning was considered unlikely because the deaths occurred across regions with vastly different vegetation types;
Rabies was excluded after multiple giraffes tested negative;
Lightning strikes were dismissed because many sick animals were seen outside rainy periods and survived for some time before dying, while lightning would have caused instant death.

Insects to blame 
Researchers noted that the outbreak followed an exceptionally good rainy season, which resulted in unusually high insect populations, particularly flies, mosquitoes and midges.

Of the 14 farmer questionnaires received, 71,4% reported significantly higher insect numbers during April and May compared with the previous year.

At the same time, several LSD outbreaks were recorded in cattle in northern Namibia.

Importantly, giraffe mortalities declined sharply with the arrival of the first winter cold spells, which also reduced insect numbers dramatically.

This strongly supports the theory that the disease was spread by insects.

Although isolated cases of LSD in giraffes have previously been reported, Namibia’s 2025 outbreak appears to have been the first largescale event of its kind.

Multiple farmers reported seeing giraffes with the typical skin lesions associated with LSD.

While sheep, goats and most African wild ungulates appear resistant to the disease, impala and springbuck may be susceptible.

What is lumpy skin disease?

Lumpy skin disease is caused by a capripox virus and results in nodular skin lesions on infected animals.

Humans cannot contract the disease.

LSD is widespread throughout Africa and has also spread into parts of Europe, Russia, the Middle East and Asia.

The disease is mainly transmitted mechanically by biting insects such as stable flies, mosquitoes and Culicoides midges.

Certain hard tick species may also spread the virus.

Direct transmission through saliva, contaminated feed or milk may occur, but this route is not yet fully understood.

Warm, wet weather creates ideal breeding conditions for insect vectors, often leading to severe outbreaks.

Long-distance spread is usually associated with the movement of infected animals rather than insects alone.

The virus is highly resilient and may survive for up to a month in dried skin crusts and up to five months in semen.

Signs and impact of LSD
Infected animals usually first develop fever, lethargy and loss of appetite.

Excessive tearing, salivation, nasal discharge and swollen lymph nodes are also common.

This is followed by the appearance of firm skin nodules ranging from 0.5 cm to 5 cm in diameter.

Lesions may occur anywhere on the body but are especially common around the head, neck, udder, scrotum and perineum.

Some animals develop only a few nodules, while others may have hundreds.

As the lesions ulcerate, fluid leaks out and secondary bacterial infections may develop, leading to pus formation and tissue death.

In severe cases, lesions can spread into the respiratory and digestive tracts.

Dairy cattle in peak production are particularly vulnerable and often suffer major drops in milk yield.

Not all infected animals show visible signs.

Up to 50% of cases during an outbreak may remain subclinical.

Mortality rates are usually below 10%, but the economic impact can still be severe due to reduced milk production, weight loss and damage to hides.

Diagnosis and reporting
LSD is a notifiable disease, meaning suspected cases must be reported to a state veterinarian.

Diagnosis is confirmed through skin samples and laboratory testing.

Other diseases can resemble LSD, including pseudo-lumpy skin disease caused by a herpesvirus and Dermatophilus congolensis infections, making laboratory confirmation essential.

PCR testing is commonly used to detect viral genetic material and identify specific virus strains.

Treatment and prevention
There is no specific cure for lumpy skin disease.

Treatment in livestock focuses on managing secondary bacterial infections, reducing fever and supporting recovery through antibiotics, anti-inflammatory medication and vitamins.

Prevention remains the most effective strategy.

Good summer rains combined with high insect numbers should alert farmers to the possibility of an outbreak.

In cattle, vaccination with the attenuated Neethling strain vaccine provides effective protection.

All cattle older than six months should be vaccinated annually, ideally before the rainy season begins.

Fly control is equally important.

Farmers are encouraged to use fly traps and apply insecticides through dipping or spraying.

Severely affected animals may need to be culled to limit spread within herds, although ------- should remember that the virus can survive for weeks in the skin of dead animals.

LSD and wildlife
Very little research exists on the role wildlife plays in the spread of LSD.

Capripoxviruses are among the most serious of all animal poxviruses.

Researchers have found antibodies against capripox viruses in species including wildebeest, springbuck, eland, impala, kudu, waterbuck, reedbuck, giraffe and African buffalo.

The presence of antibodies suggests exposure to the virus, although it does not necessarily mean the animals spread the disease further.

The actual number of infected wild animals may be much higher than reported because skin lesions are often difficult to observe in free-ranging wildlife.

Sick animals may also fall prey to predators before they can be detected.

It is well established that giraffes are susceptible to LSD.

In 1970, a giraffe calf in South Africa was experimentally infected with the virus and died with typical symptoms.

The virus has also been isolated from a giraffe that died in a Vietnamese zoo.

However, infection does not always result in death.

Mortality depends on factors such as infection severity, insect pressure and the animal’s overall condition.

Wildlife veterinarians are urging farmers to report sick or dead giraffes immediately.

Source references

# Vaccines: Lessons from the 2024 avian influenza vaccination campaign in Finland: a qualitative inquiry

Vaccines: Lessons from the 2024 avian influenza vaccination campaign in Finland: a qualitative inquiry

#19,178In 2023 Finland's fur industry was hit hard by HPAI H5N1, with more than 70 fur farms infected, and > 500,000 animals culled.

After an uncertain start, the Finnish Food Agency ordered an aggressive quarantine and culling program, which was strongly opposed by the fur industry.

Following on the heels of a mink-variant COVID outbreak in 2020, these HPAI outbreaks also brought renewed calls for the banning of the fur trade in the EU, and around the globe.

Although outbreaks reports from fur farms declined by the end of 2023, this epizootic - combined with increasing avian flu activity around the globe - led the EU to order a significant quantity of H5 vaccine the following summer.

PRESS RELEASE 11 June 2024 Brussels Commission secures access for Member States to 665,000 doses of zoonotic influenza vaccines to prevent avian fluToday, the Commission's Health Emergency Preparedness and Response Authority (HERA) as part of its mandate on preparedness, has signed on behalf of participating Member States, a joint procurement framework contract for the supply of up to 665,000 pre-pandemic vaccine doses of the up-to-date Zoonotic Influenza Vaccine Seqirus, as well as an option for a further 40 million doses over the duration of the contract.

Thanks to this contract the participating Member States will have access to medical countermeasures to prevent avian flu.

The vaccine is intended for those most exposed to potential transfers of avian influenza from birds or animals, such as poultry farm workers and -------------.

It aims to prevent the spread or potential outbreaks of avian influenza in Europe, protecting citizens and livelihoods.

The vaccine is the only preventive zoonotic avian influenza vaccine currently authorized in the EU.

(Continue . . . )

While the intent was to store these doses for use in a future outbreak, the EU carved out 20,000 doses for immediate use in Finland; specifically for the vaccination of high risk fur farmers (see Finland: MOH Announcement On Avian Flu Vaccine Availability For People At High Risk).In August of 2024, Finland's THL Announced the Start Of H5 Vaccination For High Risk Groups.

While details on the campaign were scant, last December, in a study published in Nature Microbiology, researchers discussed the disappointingly low uptake - particularly among fur farmers - of this vaccine.

The final sample size was smaller than planned due to recruitment challenges, largely reflecting the overall low vaccine uptake across all target groups.

This was particularly evident among fur farm workers, none of whom participated despite repeated outreach efforts.

I've not been able to determine how many fur farmers actually accepted the vaccine, but it appears to be a small fraction of the roughly 860 people who received as least 1 dose. All of which brings us to a new Short Communications, published in the journal Vaccines, which details the Finnish vaccination campaign and some of the perceived difficulties in convincing high-risk individuals to get the shot.

While the timing, logistics, and `divergent risk perceptions' were cited as barriers to vaccination, distrust of the government appears to have been the biggest factor.

They call it the `most unexpected barrier', but to anyone who has spent any time online - or actually talking to the public over the past 20 years - it can't have been that unexpected.

This report is based in large part on 17 interviews, of which only one was actually a farmer (poultry).

Since no fur farmers were willing to talk to these researchers, many of their conclusions are - by necessity - based on 3rd-party assessments.

Despite these limitations, this is a fascinating look at the growing chasm between governmental expectations and the public's willingness to cooperate.

I've only posted some excerpts, so follow the link to read it in its entirety.

I'll have a bit more after the break.

Short communication Lessons from the 2024 avian influenza vaccination campaign in Finland: a qualitative inquiryVuokko Härmä a, ----------------, Aapo Kuusipalo a b, -------------, -------------, ------------- a https://doi.org/10.1016/j.vaccine.2026.128736 Under a Creative Commons license AbstractHighly pathogenicity avian influenza H5N1 (HPAI H5N1) viruses cause a continuous threat to wild avian populations.

During recent years, spillover to both wild and domestic mammals has occurred with an increasing frequency.

As a consequence of the recent developments in the epidemiological situation, the human-animal interface with the risk of human exposure to HPAI H5 has expanded.

In 2024, Finland became a global forerunner to offer H5 vaccine to occupational risk groups, specifically fur and poultry workers, following an extensive HPAI H5N1 outbreak in 2023 in fur-farmed minks and foxes.

Despite targeted efforts to reach the people at increased risk, only 8,6% of the target population received the first dose and 7,5% completed both doses.

To seek a better understanding of the barriers behind low vaccine uptake a Behavioural and Cultural (BCI) insight approach was chosen.

A rapid qualitative study was conducted in late 2024 (n = 17), utilising semi-structured interviews with health authorities, industry stakeholders, and risk group representatives in the Ostrobothnia region in Finland.

Barriers were identified across three dimensions:(1) logistical failures, including poor timing and difficulties in reaching target groups(2) divergent risk perceptions, where economic livelihood overshadowed personal health risks; and (3) political distrust, stemming from perceived stigmatization by national health authorities.

The results will provide vital information for future pre-pandemic communication and implementation strategies and helps to identify key stakeholders and target groups.

(SNIP) 3.3.

Trust and political tensionsPerhaps the most unexpected barrier identified was the erosion of trust between the fur industry and national health authorities.

Our analysis suggests that the campaign's reception was influenced by ongoing political and ethical debate regarding the future of fur farming in Finland.

The 2023 outbreak led to intense public debate and strict regulatory measures, including mass culling of animals in affected farms ordered by the Finnish Food Authority as part of outbreak control measures.

Advocates of the fur industry publicly argued that such measures were excessive, and that the industry was being “scapegoated” for a natural phenomenon.

Statements from national health and political figures during the outbreak were perceived by ------- as stigmatizing.

Consequently, when these same national authorities recommended vaccination in 2024, the message was not welcomed but was met with skepticism.

A dichotomy in trust was evident, while ------- generally maintained high trust in local occupational healthcare nurses and municipal doctors, they expressed deep distrust toward national authorities.

National-level communication was characterised as bureaucratic, distant, and sometimes “accusatory”.

Participants reported that official messaging was perceived as stigmatizing for the fur farmers for the zoonotic risk, which reduced willingness to comply with voluntary measures.

Additionally, local media in the region were reportedly hesitant to publish pro-vaccination content related to avian influenza, fearing backlash from a community already feeling under siege.

This created a vacuum of positive reinforcement, further limiting the reach of health messaging.

(SNIP)4.

Conclusion and implicationsThe low uptake of the H5 vaccine in Finland serves as a critical case study in the challenges of implementing public health interventions within agricultural and occupational settings that have been involved in political tensions.

Achieving high vaccination coverage can be particularly challenging when the target population experiences a combination of marginalisation and structural distrust.

However coverage was low across all target groups, which further indicates structural and communicational challenges.

(SNIP)

While the socio-political climate of the Finnish fur industry is unique and findings should be applied cautiously to other contexts, this study offers broader insights for One Health interventions.

They demonstrate that preparedness requires more than just clinical readiness; it demands a multisectoral strategy that aligns public health goals with the economic and social realities of the agricultural sector.

Vaccination outcomes are shaped by context-specific social, political, and cultural factors, which must be considered to ensure optimal uptake.

(Continue . . .

)

While the embattled fur farming community is probably not the best proxy for attitudes of the rest of the world's population, convincing the public to take any experimental vaccine in the absence of an imminent high-morbidity/mortality outbreak is always going to be a tough sell.

Today, trust (and uptake) in vaccines has plummeted, while warnings from the WHO, or the CDC, are often dismissed as hyperbole or part of some conspiratorial `plandemic'.

Although most of this is ginned up out of whole cloth by clickbait videos and deranged social media posts, some of this mistrust has been rightfully earned (see When Scientists Behave Badly).When the next global public health threat begins its world tour, governments and health agencies are going to be desperate to find some way to regain the trust - and cooperation - of the public.

And right now, that's not a battle they appear to be even close to winning.

# Bird flu returns in Friesland and Flevoland, forcing culling of over 135,000 birds

Bird flu returns in Friesland and Flevoland, forcing culling of over 135,000 birds

Bird flu has resurfaced in the Netherlands, with outbreaks confirmed at a poultry farm in Marrum, in the province of Friesland, and earlier at a farm in Biddinghuizen, the government reports.

In total, more than 135,000 birds have been culled: about 55,000 chickens in Biddinghuizen and more than 80,000 meat chicks in Marrum.

The latest outbreak in Marrum, located in North Friesland, involves a meat chicken farm where authorities confirmed avian influenza.

The 80,000 chicks on the property are being destroyed as part of containment measures.

The new case is notable because highly contagious bird flu typically spreads mainly in the autumn and winter months, when large numbers of wild birds are migrating through the region.

On April 21, Dutch authorities lifted the national housing and shielding requirement for poultry in most of the country, concluding that the highest risk period had passed.

That measure now remains in force only in the Gelderse Vallei, an area including the municipalities of Barneveld and Lunteren, where poultry farming is heavily concentrated.

Despite that easing of restrictions, two new infections have now been confirmed.

Last week, 55,000 chickens were culled at a poultry farm in Biddinghuizen in Flevoland.

The most recent case followed in Marrum, according to Omrop Fryslân.

The outbreaks have prompted additional containment measures in Friesland.

Within a three-kilometer radius of the infected farm in Marrum, there is one other commercial poultry operation.

Within a ten-kilometer zone, there are 11 poultry farms.

Authorities have imposed a transport ban in this area covering birds, poultry manure, and eggs.

The Dutch Food and Consumer Product Safety Authority, known as NVWA, is conducting tracing investigations into what it describes as “risky contacts,” examining whether poultry or poultry products were transported to or from the Marrum farm in the period before the infection was detected.

fulltext:
# Why Europe’s First Avian Influenza Vaccination Campaign Stalled?

Why Europe’s First Avian Influenza Vaccination Campaign Stalled?

25 May 2026
Following a massive 2023 highly pathogenic avian influenza (HPAI) H5N1 outbreak across 71 fur farms that exposed over 150 people, Finland became the first country to offer a targeted H5 vaccine to fur and poultry workers in July 2024.

Despite securing 20,000 doses for an estimated 10,000 at-risk individuals, the voluntary campaign faced steep resistance.

According to the official Finnish vaccination register, between July 2024 and October 2025: - 8.6% of the target population received the first dose. -

Only 7.5% completed the full two-dose series.

To understand these barriers, researchers from the Finnish Institute for Health and Welfare (THL) and the University of Eastern Finland conducted 17 semi-structured interviews with health experts, authorities, and industry stakeholders in the Ostrobothnia region.

The thematic analysis highlighted three primary dimensions that derailed the intervention: The rollout launched during peak summer vacations, causing severe healthcare staffing shortages that prevented the use of mobile clinics.

Organizers also lacked centralized registries to effectively reach seasonal or foreign laborers.

While authorities focused on pandemic prevention and personal health, farmers prioritized economic survival.

Recovering from the 2023 crisis, their main anxiety was financial ruin from potential mass animal cullings.

Because the human vaccine could not protect their livestock or prevent culling orders, interest plummeted.

Knowledge gaps also caused some to believe the vaccine was for animals, or to view the rapid rollout as an experiment.

A severe erosion of trust occurred between the fur industry and national health authorities following mandatory animal cullings in 2023.

Industry advocates felt scapegoated and stigmatized by bureaucratic national communications.

Consequently, while farmers highly trusted local municipal doctors and occupational nurses, they rejected voluntary guidelines issued by national bodies.

Perhaps this failure was foreseeable, given that the initiative to vaccinate these designated at-risk workers lacked solid scientific data demonstrating that individuals in direct contact with infected mink or poultry had actually contracted or fallen ill from the avian influenza virus.

Härmä et al.

(2026).

Lessons from the 2024 avian influenza vaccination campaign in Finland: a qualitative inquiry.

Vaccine, 86, 128736.

# The "six-year cycle of pandemics" is gaining popularity on social networks, but experts refute it.

The "six-year cycle of pandemics" is gaining popularity on social networks, but experts refute it.

Epidemiologists deny rumours of a "secret cycle of pandemics" in the world – Photo: AFP In Indonesia, a video is circulating on social media claiming that viral outbreaks or pandemics occur in the world every six years, and even implying that these outbreaks were "deliberately planned".

However, epidemiologists argue that there is no scientific evidence to support this hypothesis.

Accordingly, a video posted on Instagram listed a number of major epidemics, such as SARS in 2002, H1N1 flu in 2009, Ebola, COVID-19 and hantavirus, to create the illusion of a fixed "pandemic cycle".

However, epidemiologist Dickie Budiman of Griffith University said that the history of outbreaks shows that the intervals between epidemics vary greatly and do not obey the six-year rule.

As examples, he cited SARS (2002), H1N1 influenza (2009), Middle East respiratory syndrome (MERS) (2012) and COVID-19 (2019).

In addition, Ebola, Zika, avian influenza and hantavirus viruses have appeared at different times.

According to Budiman, the deliberate timing of certain time frames while ignoring other outbreaks created the illusion of a "secret pattern."

He stressed that the Ebola virus was actually discovered in the 1970s, while the Hunt virus was known from the 1950s to the 1970s and is not a recent phenomenon.

The expert said that scientists estimate that there are approximately 1.6 million types of viruses in nature, and about 800,000 of them are capable of causing diseases in humans.

However, mankind has thoroughly studied only a very small part of them.

Experts also warn that people often tend to look for patterns in random events — a phenomenon known as apophenia, that is, seeing connections even when there's no clear cause-and-effect relationship.

Scientists also advise the public to be cautious about information about the pandemic spread on social media, as conspiracy theories related to the pandemic can easily cause panic and distort scientific understanding.

According to historical documents published on the scientific platform ScienceDirect, infectious diseases have existed throughout human history, from the Athenian plague of 430 BC to the Plague of Justinian in 541 and many major outbreaks in the modern era.

Experts say that instead of believing in "pandemic cycles", it is much more important to strengthen epidemiological surveillance, virus research and build global health capacity to reduce the risk of future outbreaks.

Let's get back to the topic.
