# How WOAH's new and revised chapters ensure better control of avian influenza

How WOAH's new and revised chapters ensure better control of avian influenza

The 93rd General Session of the World Organisation for Animal Health has marked the revision of WOAH’s standard on trade and the creation of a whole new resolution on biosecurity.

The decision was driven by requests from WOAH members to include information on biosecurity in the standards.

Like all other standards designed by WOAH for Members to implement, the ones that dominated this year’s agenda are poised to have a positive impact on animal health and welfare worldwide.

Among the areas where they bring wide-ranging benefits, the live bird market landscape, where infectious diseases like avian influenza may spread, stands out as a particularly vulnerable yet crucially central one.

Understanding the issue through the lens of the commodity
Live bird markets have traditionally played a key role in southeast Asia and Western Africa.

In some countries, they are the backbone of cities that rely on food-focused trade and tourism.

In most of them, live bird markets ensure food security by providing access to affordable fresh produce and embody a stable source of income for the many vendors that populate this vibrant community space.

Infectious animal diseases like avian influenza, however, have put these settings under significant strain, as they offer a breeding ground for the emergence and spread of harmful pathogens.

When zoonotic diseases emerge, outbreaks affect poultry trade and cause loss of consumer confidence.

Wet markets where live animals are sold and handled are often linked to the transmission of zoonotic diseases, as they can facilitate the movement of live animals that may carry pathogens.

Interspecies mixing in wet markets further complicates the game, posing significant health and ecological risks.

Too often, veterinary public health interventions aimed at curbing the spread of the virus have focused on tackling the disease strictly within the borders of the markets themselves.

Measures have included banning the storage of birds overnight in markets, enforcing market ‘rest days’ and separating bird species in markets.

In the wake of the COVID-19 pandemic, for instance, several countries temporarily shut down their wet markets which stalled trade flaws and critical food supply chains.

But this solution proved neither sustainable nor viable in the long term.

It triggered unintended consequences, such as the emergence of unregulated black markets for animal products and the attribution of the pandemic to these cultural practices.

The stigma now attached to “wet markets” has become heavily laden with negative connotations.

By contrast, evidence shows that avian influenza control for pandemic prevention must start before poultry reaches wet markets.

Indeed, measures focused on live bird markets only are unlikely to effectively reduce the exposure of market traders and shoppers to avian influenza risk if not compounded by ad hoc interventions along the supply chain.

Pandemic prevention strategies must therefore target chicken farmers and transporters in countries where the virus is endemic to reduce the number of infected chickens introduced into markets.

Shaping the future of live poultry markets and beyond  
Various studies have identified the main risks associated with these settings.

Markets selling live animals pose the greatest risks to human health and biodiversity due to their pathogenic load – especially those involving wild animals, which are intimately connected to emerging infectious diseases.

Factors in combination, such as the presence of live animals, including those presenting a high risk of diseases, market size and cleanliness and hygienic practices could contribute to disease emergence.

Given the complex nature of infectious disease risks, policymakers should not focus solely on markets when mitigating future outbreaks.

Targeted, science-informed, and risk-based interventions applied upstream in the supply chain as well as in cross-border movements of birds can make bird markets safer without bringing local economies to the brink of collapse.

Poor biosecurity and unregulated trade are part of the problem as they can exacerbate disease risks.

This is why the revision of Article 5.7.1. of WOAH’s Code and the inclusion of a whole new chapter on biosecurity during this year’s General Session mark a step towards a healthier, disease-free animal populations.

When it comes to live bird markets, animals are sourced domestically.

Yet, regardless of where they are sourced, it is important to always implement effective biosecurity from the farm of origin to the market.

Being the cornerstone of animal health programmes, biosecurity should be implemented to prevent and control diseases in populations.

Together with the recent recommendations on border inspection posts and quarantine centres to support effective implementation of measures and procedures applicable to the transit, import and export of commodities, these approaches reduce the introduction of infection to populations and support trade continuity.

Targeted One Health interventions 
Besides standards, stakeholder collaboration needs to the put into practice in this complex context.

The One Health approach aims to prevent zoonotic diseases by addressing risks at their source, such as in wet markets, through multidisciplinary collaboration among public health experts, veterinarians and environmental specialists to ensure safe food practices and effective regulation of outlets where animals and food are sold.

One Health solutions could include infrastructure improvements, regular cleaning and “rest days” to interrupt viral circulation, vendor-centric training programmes on hygiene and infection prevention, monitoring and certification systems that reward compliance rather than penalise trade.

This combination of approaches can help achieve a broader, important end-goal: resilient, safe bird markets that sustain livelihoods while protecting against the looming threat of avian influenza.

Targeted interventions and application of standards, not blanket bans, offer a pragmatic path toward safer animal trade practices.

Members are invited to diligently implement and adapt WOAH’s standards to their legislation to make sure that the disease risk is mitigated today – and everyone’s health is protected tomorrow.

# What to Know About the Current State of Bird Flu in the U.S.: A Conversation with -----------

What to Know About the Current State of Bird Flu in the U.S.: A Conversation with -----------

What is the current risk of an H5N1 outbreak in the U.S., and how concerned should we be?Gibbs:

We’re already witnessing H5N1 outbreaks in dairy cows and bird flocks all around the country.

But right now, we’re fortunate that most human cases of H5N1 are associated with farm workers who have only had mild symptoms and are younger and healthier.

So, people are not taking this seriously, which increases the potential for illness to move into the community.

And the more people that a virus infects, the more likely it is to mutate.

Identifying and reporting these outbreaks can be one of the bigger obstacles to rein in an epidemic before it occurs.

However, not everyone will be actively testing their workers for H5N1, and working while sick is very common.

A lot of farm workers don’t actively seek care or treatment unless it’s a catastrophic or a near-catastrophic case because they don’t want to stop working and lose income.

What other species might be affected?Gibbs: The virus is working [its way] through poultry, and now through dairy cows — which is something that we haven’t seen until the past few years.

This created a lot of consternation with both practitioners and researchers as to where we may see more disease exposure.

Workers must also be trained on how they interact with other animals that may not be clear carriers of H5N1 — such as rodents and cats.

We’ve seen a lot of farm cats dying from H5N1.And we just don’t have good surveillance on what this is doing in the farm worker population and what it’s doing in the general human population.

Farm workers can protect themselves with personal protective equipment (PPE) such as masks, gloves, and goggles.

But a lot of workers are reluctant to use all that gear.

What could be done to encourage them?Gibbs: Imagine wearing a N95 filtering face mask and goggles while working in a farm setting.

Your biggest concern is, “If this animal steps on --, it will break my foot,

[and] - won’t be able to work or get paid.”

Wearing the PPE not only can make --- uncomfortable, it may impact your situational awareness and make it harder to avoid being stepped on.

But if --- don’t wear the N95 filtering face mask and goggles, --- may get sick.

In that moment, that trade-off feels more practical to protect your short-term health than for --- to protect your longer-term health, or even public health in that community.

We need to facilitate innovation around PPE.

We need to develop PPE that’s more comfortable, doesn’t fog up, is easier to use, and can be maintained on the facility.

Right now, this kind of equipment doesn’t exist, and if it did, it’s not cost-effective.

Another major barrier is the behavioral stigma around PPE use — we need to convince farm workers to use PPE and show them how to use it so it does not negatively impact their ability to work.

How should we prepare for the possibility that H5N1 becomes endemic in livestock?Gibbs: Now that H5N1 has gone through dairy facilities several times, producers recognize and treat it and do their best to isolate and prevent exposure.

At this point, dairy producers are now dealing with H5N1 as a somewhat-known entity, and some likely on an annual basis — which can negatively impact public health and the nation’s ability to understand what’s happening because they aren’t necessarily reporting it like they did when it first appeared.

--------- aren’t doing anything wrong.

Just like you

and - aren’t going to the doctor for the flu each time we get sick, producers know dairy cows can be treated and survive, so they are less likely to report it.

In birds, they essentially have to depopulate the entire flock, whereas in cattle, they treat the cattle and keep them away from the other livestock.

They have pretty good systems in place to treat the dairy cows or to depopulate the poultry.

However, we are still missing good monitoring systems for both animals and humans that can be implemented in conjunction with industry.

How can local health departments build trust with farm owners and workers so that reporting can be improved and outbreaks can be prevented?Gibbs: Public health organizations across the country need to prioritize building trust within these communities and partnering with organizations such as agriculture extension (the network of research institutions, universities, and local governments that support farm performance and rural development) that are already valuable, trusted partners to many in these communities.

Working more with them can support stronger response to potential outbreaks.

If there were funding and support for it, either from the state or the federal level, then that’s money well invested in our future, because H5N1 exposure poses a national security issue.

Feeding the population is incredibly important.

We want to make sure that we can continue to do that — and a major part of that means healthy animals, healthy workers, and a healthy environment.

What are the most important steps to contain H5N1 going forward?Gibbs: A major risk around H5N1 is that it will not go away, but it can be forgotten about.

As a general population, we need to ensure we continue monitoring, because while the virus isn’t having huge negative impacts on affected workers, the virus will still be here.

If it were to get into the general population, which has more vulnerable and susceptible individuals, we would likely see more adverse health impacts.

Also, in a larger population, it could be more likely to mutate.

We must figure out a system to monitor infections in farm animals and ------- that will be palatable to the producers, -------, and overall communities so everyone feels empowered to share information.

With an appropriate system, we can continue to protect the health of the animals, the -------, and the communities.

# Eurosurveillance: Cross-reactive human antibody responses to H9N2 influenza virus, New York, United States, 2025

Eurosurveillance: Cross-reactive human antibody responses to H9N2 influenza virus, New York, United States, 2025

Credit NIAID #19,172While LPAI H9N2 remains far from our biggest pandemic concern, the CDC has designated 2 different lineages (A(H9N2) G1 and A(H9N2) Y280) as having at least some pandemic potential (see CDC IRAT SCORE), and several candidate vaccines have been developed.

All of which makes it important to know how much existing immunity their might be in the community should LPAI H9N2 ever embark on a world tour; particularly from regions of the world where the virus has not been circulating.

To that end we have the following Rapid Communication - published yesterday in the journal Eurosurveillance - which looks at a relatively small cohort (n=298) of serum samples collected in NYC between between Feb 2024 and Apr 2025, which they tested for signs of pre-existing immunity against 1 specific strain (A/Changsha/SR353/2025 - Y280 lineage) of LPAI H9N2.

In brief, they found widespread - but weak - cross-reactive haemagglutinin (HA)-binding antibodies among the 298 samples, likely due to previous seasonal flu vaccinations and/or exposures (see comparison chart below).On the other hand, neuraminidase-inhibiting antibodies were more common and stronger.

All this suggests that while protection against H9 infection is likely very limited - and unlikely to create much of a barrier for the spread of the virus - the stronger NA antibodies may help reduce the severity of infection.

At least that's the hope.

Interestingly, there is a small but perceptible dip in both HA and NA inhibition activity in those born in the mid-to-late 1980s and early 1990s, but given the small sample size and weak response, this may not be significant.

Although the news isn't all bad, this study looks at only one small and geographically limited dataset - and one specific strain of LPAI H9N2 - so there is only so much we can infer from their findings.

I've only posted the link and some excerpts below, most will want to follow the link to read the report in its entirety.

Open AccessCross-reactive human antibody responses to H9N2 influenza virus, New York, United States, 2025 Gagandeep Singh1,2 , Disha Bhavsar1,2 , Lucas M Ferreri3 , Jessica R Nardulli1,2 , Charles Gleason1,2 , Neko Lyttle1,2 , Yuexing Chen1,2 , Anice C Lowen3 , Viviana Simon1,2,4,5,6 , Florian Krammer1,2,6,7,8  Avian influenza A(H9N2) viruses have been endemic in poultry for many years across Asia, the Middle East, and Northern and Western Africa and have been detected periodically in the Americas and Europe.

Since their first detection in 1966, H9N2 viruses have evolved into multiple lineages and exhibit a high propensity for reassortment, contributing internal genes to zoonotic viruses such as H5N1, H7N9 and H10N8

[1,2].Human infections with H9N2 viruses have been reported since 1998, with most cases linked to poultry exposure and typically resulting in mild or asymptomatic disease but severe cases and deaths have been observed as well.

Despite these observations, population-level immunity to H9N2 remains poorly defined, particularly in regions without endemic exposure to the virus.

Here, we assessed cross-reactive antibody responses to H9N2 viruses using a panel of recently collected human sera from the general population of a large metropolitan city in North America (New York City)        (SNIP)Our work describes pre-existing immunity at population level to avian influenza A(H9N2) viruses in adults from New York City.

We observed widespread cross-reactive haemagglutinin (HA)-binding antibodies in the sampled group; however, these responses were of lower magnitude than those against seasonal H3.

Consistent with this, HI and neutralising antibody responses to H9N2 were low or undetectable in most individuals, indicating limited functional immunity.

In contrast, a high proportion of individuals exhibited N2-binding and NI antibodies, including cross-reactive responses to H9N2.

Although NA-directed antibodies do not prevent infection, they may reduce viral replication and disease severity, potentially providing the population with some degree of resilience against emerging influenza viruses [6,7].

The comparable magnitude of NI responses to H9N2 and H3N2 suggests that prior exposure to seasonal influenza viruses has generated cross-reactive NA immunity.

This is consistent with historical observations that pre-existing anti-N2 antibodies, induced during circulation of A(H2N2) viruses, may have contributed to reduced disease severity during the emergence of the A(H3N2) pandemic

[17,18].The difference between HA and NA responses is not surprising.

Comparative sequence analysis revealed substantial divergence between the HA proteins of lineage B4.7.2 virus A/Changsha/SR353/2025 (H9N2) and seasonal A/Thailand/8/2022 (H3N2), with only approximately 40% amino acid identity.

In contrast, the N2 neuraminidases of the two viruses showed high conservation, sharing approximately 89% amino-acid identity.

Our study also has several limitations.

Vaccination data were not captured, and we therefore cannot draw any conclusions about the effect of seasonal vaccination on H9N2 immunity.

Furthermore, our analysis was restricted to a small, geographically limited group of individuals.

Finally, different lineages of H9N2 circulate and they are antigenically distinct.

This study only used one of these viruses as reagent and cross-reactive responses to other lineages of H9N2 may differ.

ConclusionIn summary, we found that pre-existing immunity to H9N2 in humans that is dominated by cross-reactive but largely non-neutralising antibodies, with a greater contribution from NA-directed responses.

Given the pandemic potential of H9N2 viruses, these findings have important implications for risk assessment and support the inclusion of NA immunity in vaccine development strategies.

Further studies are needed to confirm whether cross-reactive NI antibodies confer protection against H9N2 infection or modulate disease severity, but our results suggest that the antibodies against H9N2 virus might derive from exposure to conserved epitopes shared between the avian-origin and seasonal strains.

# Vaccination for avian flu

Vaccination for avian flu

Since May 2026, the Ministry of Health has launched a pilot vaccination project against highly pathogenic avian influenza, in collaboration with the Veneto and Lombardy regions, on a limited number of farms in the provinces of Verona and Mantua, a project concerning turkeys for meat and laying hens.

Calls to boycott companies participating in this pilot project have begun to appear on social media, indicating AIA as the only brand currently to be boycotted.

But the Ministry's managerial device identifies exactly 5 farms involved: 3 of broiler turkeys in the province of Verona and 2 of laying hens in the province of Mantua.

AIA does not know, nor does it emerge from any official source, that it is the only company to take part.

As reported by Telenuovo's Veronese TG, from 2020 to the beginning of 2026 there were 477 outbreaks of highly pathogenic avian influenza in Italian farms.

Numbers that have pushed towards overcoming emergency management based on culling, focusing on prevention and biosecurity, as also highlighted by the Istituto Zooprofilattico Sperimentale delle Venezie.

Those who push to boycott a single operator by explicitly naming him, while remaining silent about all the other participants in the same project, are at least exposing themselves to a reflection on the issue of unfair competition.

But it is not unfair competition that we want to talk about today, but about vaccines and avian flu.

The very first thing to say is that the messages calling for a boycott of AIA are pure misinformation, because we are talking about an mRNA vaccine, but the management device of the Ministry of Health that gives authorization to this vaccination test phase clearly states which vaccine can be used in the five farms, and it is not mRNA, but:
Beef turkeys
a) First vaccination: recombinant vaccine authorised for turkey with turkey herpesvirus (HVT) vector expressing the hemagglutinin H5 gene, administered in a hatchery on the first day of life.

(b) Second vaccination (booster): Inactivated vaccine authorised for turkey with subunits for H5, containing only the key protein of the virus (haemagglutinin, HA) and not the whole virus, administered at 30 to 36 days of age.

Pullets
a) First vaccination: recombinant vaccine authorised for chicken with HVT vector expressing hemagglutinin H5, administered in a hatchery on the first day of life.

(b) Second vaccination (booster): Inactivated H5 subunit vaccine authorised for chicken, containing only the key protein of the virus (haemagglutinin, HA) and not the whole virus, administered between 10 and 12 weeks of age.

The recombinant vaccine
It is a technology in use in veterinary medicine since the nineties, so if you worry today it is definitely late.

But to fully clarify let's try to understand what we are talking about.

This type of vaccine works like this: you take a virus that is already known and harmless to humans – in this case it is the turkey herpesvirus, which has been used for decades as a vaccine against Marek's disease – and insert the gene that codes for hemagglutinin H5, the surface protein of the avian flu virus.

The vector enters the animal's cells and produces that protein, training the immune system to recognize it.

No live influenza viruses are inoculated, no mRNA, and no modification to the animal's DNA occurs.

All very simple, and the booster of the second vaccination is even simpler since it is an inactivated vaccine that contains only the purified protein, without any genetic material.

Those who talk about "chicken mRNA" vaccination either don't know what they're talking about or are riding on misinformation to stimulate the indignation of an easily suggestible public.

As we have been saying for some time, fear is very useful when trying to influence public opinion.

Wrapping up
If I were in the Ministry of Health, I would immediately trigger complaints for procured alarm, as well as for possible damage to the companies mentioned.

But -'m just a fact checker.

# CDC: Enhanced Ebola Airport Screening Begins at Washington-Dulles International Airport

CDC: Enhanced Ebola Airport Screening Begins at Washington-Dulles International Airport

#19,171As we discussed 3 days ago in CDC Statement on the Use of Public Health Travel Restrictions to Prevent the Introduction of Ebola Disease into the United States, we possess no technology that permits us to reliably screen presymptomatic travelers entering the United States for infectious diseases.

Temperature screening and health questionnaires can help identify some infected individuals - but with incubation periods that can range from days to weeks - only a small percentage of cases will likely be detected.

There are obvious advantages to identifying (and isolating) those already showing signs of illness, as they are most likely to be infectious.

But once a virus becomes sufficiently transmissible among humans, the best we can hope for is to slow the spread (see Why Airport Screening Can’t Stop MERS, Ebola or Avian Flu).Different diseases spread at different rates, and luckily Ebola (like Hantavirus) requires far closer, and more prolonged, contact than highly contagious pathogens like measles or influenza.

That doesn't render them incapable of international spread, but neither have shown the ability to spread like a classic pandemic virus (with the caveat that viral evolution means that whatever we say today may not necessarily hold true tomorrow).

Yesterday the CDC announced plans to redirect returning American travelers who have recently (within 21 days) visited the DRC, Uganda, or South Sudan to Washington Dulles International Airport, where they will be screened for potential Ebola exposure.

Those who are asymptomatic will be provided health information - and told what to do if symptoms should later appear - then allowed to proceed to their final destinations.

Those who are symptomatic will be evaluated by a CDC health officer.

Should conditions warrant, there is always the option of adjusting these screening protocols.

The CDC's full statement follows:Enhanced Ebola Airport Screening Begins at Washington-Dulles International AirportStatementFor immediate release: May 21, 2026CDC Media Relations(404) 639-3286On May 18, 2026, the CDC issued an Order suspending entry to the United States of foreign nationals who were in the Democratic Republic of Congo (DRC), Uganda, or South Sudan within 21 days before arrival.

U.S. citizens, U.S. nationals, and lawful permanent residents who were in these countries within 21 days are permitted to enter the United States.

The Department of Homeland Security (DHS) posted a Federal Register notice, available here, that outlines the redirection of these affected travelers to Washington-Dulles International Airport (IAD) beginning 11:59 p.m.

May 20, 2026, for enhanced public health screening.

CDC will conduct the enhanced public health entry screenings for these travelers and confirm their contact information for public health follow-up if recommended.

Travelers that have been in the DRC, Uganda, and South Sudan in the 21 days before their flight, and are scheduled to travel to the United States, while screening is taking place will be contacted by their airline to rebook travel to IAD.To date, no suspected, probable, or confirmed cases of Ebola have been reported in the United States, and the risk of Ebola domestically is low.

However, public health entry screening is part of a layered approach that, when used with other public health measures already in place to detect symptomatic arriving travelers, can slow and reduce the spread of disease into the United States.

How Public Health Entry Screening WorksTravelers who were in DRC, Uganda, or South Sudan in the 21 days before arriving to the United States will be escorted to an area of the airport set aside for screening.

--------- will respond to a brief questionnaire that asks about their travel history and symptoms, and collects information so the --------- can be contacted, if needed.

CDC staff will observe these --------- for signs of illness and take ---------' temperatures using non-contact thermometers (thermometers that do not touch the skin).Travelers who do not have symptoms, but have been in DRC, Uganda, or South Sudan in the past 21 days will be given information on monitoring their health and actions to take if symptoms later appear.

These --------- will continue to their final destinations.

Traveler contact information will be shared with state and local health departments for additional follow-up and support.

If a traveler has a fever or other symptoms, the -------- will be evaluated by a CDC public health officer.

CDC partners with state and local public health authorities and designated hospitals within those states to work quickly to identify and take appropriate public health actions for persons who present with symptoms of illness in the community.

Working together, U.S. federal, state, and local authorities are ready to manage --------- who become ill after they reach their destinations.

If a suspect case is identified, CDC will work with state/local health departments to conduct routine contact investigations to notify passengers.

CDC is prepared to provide additional resources in response to this Ebola outbreak in DRC and Uganda.

The latest information on this outbreak is available here.

# Household controls! The crime sanctioned with 1,200 lei!

Household controls!

The crime sanctioned with 1,200 lei!

------------ – Agrointelligence
May 22, 2026 11:13

DSVSA controls in households

Fines of 3,600 lei and warnings after controls in livestock farms.

In a single week, the official veterinarians from the DSVSA Satu Mare – Official Control Service for Animal Health and Welfare carried out a number of 79 controls in professional and non-professional farms, markets, veterinary sanitary offices, veterinary pharmacies.

Following the recent controls carried out by DSVSA Satu Mare, 5 contravention sanctions were applied, 3 contravention fines in the total amount of – 3,600 lei, as follows: a contravention fine in the amount of 1,200 lei for a non-professional farm in Eriu Sâncrai, a contravention fine in the amount of 1,200 lei for a non-professional farm in Unimăt,  a contravention fine in the amount of 1,200 lei for a non-professional farm in Ghirișa locality and two warnings in two non-farm farms in Căpleni and Tiream, for the sale or alienation of animals in any way, without the documents provided by the veterinary sanitary legislation in force, according to a press release.

Read also: ---------------, president of ANSVSA, message on the Day of the Veterinarian: "Veterinary medicine represents a noble vocation, unceasingly dedicated to the common good"
Controls on livestock farms
Veterinarians, on alert for the "control" of possible outbreaks of serious diseases

According to the data, in the European Union, Avian Influenza evolves – in primary or secondary outbreaks, both in poultry farms and in the wild area in: Belgium, Czech Republic, Germany, Poland, Denmark, France and Norway.

In the same period, it evolves in the E.U. – African Swine Fever, in outbreaks in: Poland, Romania, Lithuania, Latvia, Germany, Croatia, Spain, Italy, Slovakia, Moldova, Estonia, Bosnia and Herzegovina and Hungary.

In Satu Mare County, there are veterinary sanitary restrictions, due to the outbreaks of African Swine Fever in Amati and Turț localities.

DSVSA Satu Mare, has started the program of inspection actions in animal farms in the county.

These actions aim to carry out surveillance to identify animals with clinical signs of transmissible diseases in cattle, sheep, goats and pigs, existing in non-professional households.

The authorized veterinarians will evaluate the animals existing on each holding.

Animal owners have the obligation to allow access to the concessionaire veterinarians on their own farms and to ensure the containment of the kept animals, for a good and correct evaluation.

Read also:

Sheepdogs who had smallpox on farms were left without animals, and without compensation!

Sheep and goat pox in two counties
The appearance of outbreaks of sheep and goat pox in counties in Romania – Bistrita Nasaud and Mureș – obliges DSVSA Satu Mare to warn all breeders of these species in the county about the special danger posed by the appearance and evolution of this disease in livestock, the institution also notes.

Sheep and goat pox is a highly contagious viral disease caused by a virus of the Poxviridae family (genus Capripoxvirus), which affects small ruminants, causing skin lesions, fever and high mortality, especially in lambs (up to 50–70%).

Although it is not transmissible to humans, the disease causes major economic losses.

The disease is manifested by a series of specific clinical symptoms, easily recognizable by farmers:
– Skin lesions, especially around the muzzle, nose, ears, eyelids, abdomen, legs and genitals;
– High fever (40-42°C) in the first days after infection;
– Decreased appetite and weight loss;
– Lethargy and apathy;
– Excessive lacrimation and nasal secretions, sometimes purulent;
– Inflammation of the lymph nodes;
– Lesions on the oral, nasal and ocular mucous membranes, which can cause breathing and feeding difficulties.

# Phu Tho: Strengthening disease prevention and control measures for aquatic animals and aquatic animals.

Phu Tho: Strengthening disease prevention and control measures for aquatic animals and aquatic animals.

Delegates attending the conference.

Therefore, it is necessary to strengthen the synchronized implementation of different solutions to promptly prevent, control and manage outbreaks, minimizing losses for livestock farmers.

At the conference, ------------, deputy director of the Department of Agriculture and Environment of Phu Tho province, pointed out that in 2025, the growth rate of the province's livestock industry will reach only 3.3%, failing to fully exploit the huge potential of the area.

The main reason is the impact of the extended outbreak of African swine fever.

In 2026, the disease situation remains complex, with the emergence of new variants of the foot-and-mouth disease virus, which pose a high risk of re-entry.

Therefore, functional units and communes/wards are focusing on strengthening disease prevention and control measures for aquatic animals and aquatic animals.

According to the Department of Agriculture and Environment of Phu Tho Province, lately, the prevention and control of animal diseases in the area has benefited from decisive action from all levels and sectors, as well as the consensus of the population.

The province has issued numerous directives on the prevention and control of animal diseases, especially dangerous infectious diseases such as rabies, African swine fever, avian influenza and lumpy dermatosis in cattle and buffalo...

At the same time, the Provincial Department of Agriculture and Environment issued guidance on outbreak management procedures; allowed the use of disinfectants to treat high-risk areas; and set up inspection teams to monitor and oversee disease prevention and control efforts at the local level.

The Sub-Department for Animal Husbandry, Veterinary Medicine and Fisheries coordinated activities with communes and sectors to guide the population in implementing disease prevention measures, closely monitor the disease situation and promptly report any signs that appeared.

As for rabies, the authorities carried out inspections and ordered the implementation of prevention and control measures in the localities where positive samples appeared.

The province has released 1,600 doses of rabies vaccine from its reserves to deal with the outbreak.

As a result, all 1,600 dogs and cats eligible for vaccination in the affected areas have been vaccinated, achieving a vaccination rate of 100%, helping to improve community immunity in animals and limiting the risk of transmission of the disease to humans.

As for African swine fever, specialized agencies promptly detected and guided the management of outbreaks in the area.

The Provincial Department of Agriculture and Environment has provided 600 liters of disinfectant free of charge from the province's reserves to support localities in environmental treatment and disinfection of livestock farms.

According to the assessments of the relevant agencies, the situation of African swine fever is currently under good control.

To proactively secure the resources needed to respond to disease outbreaks, Phu Tho province recommended allocating the budget for the purchase and storage of vaccines and disinfectants for disease prevention and control.

Currently, the province maintains a reserve of about 60,000 doses of foot-and-mouth disease vaccine, 1 million doses of avian influenza vaccine, 10,000 doses of nodular dermatitis vaccine, 50,000 doses of rabies vaccine, and about 15,000 liters of disinfectant.

In addition to disease prevention and control efforts in animals and poultry, the agricultural sector is also strengthening its disease surveillance in aquaculture.

According to the plan, environmental monitoring and disease surveillance activities will be carried out three times a year in areas with concentrated aquaculture.

At the conference, delegates also discussed various issues, such as: continued support from specialized agencies for vaccines and disinfectants; improved technical training to enable units to proactively and promptly manage outbreaks; aquaculture facilities need to adjust feed quantities, control sources of organic waste entering ponds and increase the use of microbial products to improve water quality; and regularly monitor the health of fish stocks to detect early signs of abnormalities and take prompt action.

Farmers were encouraged to supplement vitamins and minerals to increase the resilience of aquatic animals during transition periods and prolonged heatwaves.

The provincial Department of Agriculture and Environment has identified vaccination, disinfection, proactive disease prevention and control, and rapid outbreak management

Inspection Team No 2 of the Department of Agriculture and Environment of Hanoi conducted a surprise inspection of the origin and sources of pork and chicken meat at the stalls of the Thuong Thanh Market (Vinh Hung District).

# Fines in Satu Mare for the sale of animals

Fines in Satu Mare for the sale of animals

DSVSA Satu Mare inspectors carried out, between May 15 and 22, 2026, almost 100 controls in animal farms, markets, veterinary sanitary offices and veterinary pharmacies in the county.

The checks concerned the field of animal health and welfare, in the context in which there are sanitary-veterinary restrictions at the county level due to outbreaks of African swine fever, and outbreaks of sheep and goat pox have been reported in other counties in the country.

According to DSVSA Satu Mare, this week 96 controls were carried out in professional and non-professional farms, markets, veterinary sanitary offices and veterinary pharmacies.

Following the checks, the inspectors applied 5 contravention sanctions, of which 3 fines totaling 4,800 lei and 2 warnings.

The sanctions were applied for the sale or alienation of animals without the documents provided by the sanitary-veterinary legislation in force.

Thus, a non-professional farm in Rătești was fined 2,400 lei, and two other non-professional farms, in Dobra and Borlesti localities, were fined 1,200 lei each.

Also, two warnings were applied in farms in Turț and Portița.

DSVSA Satu Mare draws attention to the fact that there are still sanitary-veterinary restrictions in the county caused by outbreaks of African swine fever in Amați, Turț, Resighea, Gereușa and Porumbești.

In this context, the institution continues the program of inspections in the county's animal farms, for the surveillance of cattle, sheep, goats and pigs from the population's households.

------------------------ will assess the animals on each holding.

Animal owners have the obligation to allow the access of concession veterinarians to households and to ensure the containment of the kept animals, so that the assessment can be done correctly.

At European level, according to data provided by DSVSA, avian influenza evolves in 49 primary or secondary outbreaks, both in poultry farms and in the wild, in several states, including Austria, Estonia, Germany, Poland, Denmark, Finland, the Netherlands and Norway.

Also in the European Union, African swine fever evolves in 249 outbreaks, including in Romania, but also in countries such as Poland, Lithuania, Latvia, Germany, Croatia, Spain, Italy, Slovakia, Moldova, Serbia, Ukraine and Hungary.

Another important warning concerns sheep and goat breeders in Satu Mare County.

DSVSA warns about the danger posed by sheep and goat pox, after the appearance of outbreaks in Bistrita-Nasaud and Mureș counties.

Sheep and goat pox is a highly contagious viral disease that affects small ruminants.

The disease is not transmitted to humans, but it can cause major economic losses and high mortality, especially among lambs.

Among the symptoms that should attract the attention of breeders are skin lesions, especially around the muzzle, nose, ears, eyelids, abdomen, legs and genitals, high fever, decreased appetite, weight loss, apathy, nasal and tear secretions, inflammation of the lymph nodes, as well as lesions on the oral, nasal and ocular mucous membranes.

DSVSA Satu Mare recommends animal owners to immediately notify the veterinarian in case of signs of illness and to comply with the measures ordered by the authorities.

For notifications or complaints in the sanitary-veterinary and food safety field, citizens can call the Call Center at 0800 826 787 or DSVSA Satu Mare, at 0261 715 956 and 0760 789 016.

# The mystery of dead pigeons in Most has been clarified. Rat poison was behind the mass death

The mystery of dead pigeons in Most has been clarified.

Rat poison was behind the mass death

The results were confirmed by specialized toxicological analyses, which the city had processed after all common bird diseases were gradually ruled out.

According to the city police management, the suspicion that human activity was behind the death, not illness or natural causes, was confirmed.

The case was extremely sensitive from the beginning.

Dead pigeons appeared in streets, parks and housing estates, and their number grew rapidly.

In a short period of time, there were hundreds of pieces that police officers and technical service workers had to remove daily.

The situation caused concerns not only about the health of other animals, but also about people, especially children and dog owners.

Diseases not confirmed, the trail led to a toxic substance
In the first phase, attention was focused on the possibility of a dangerous infection.

The Regional Veterinary Administration and hygienists checked the occurrence of bird flu or paramyxovirosis, known as pigeon plague.

However, these variants have been clearly ruled out by laboratory tests.

When even widespread tests for less common diseases failed, the city financed other, more expensive toxicological analyses.

It was these that eventually revealed the presence of rodenticides in the bodies of dead birds.

What was striking for ------- was the selectivity of death.

The poisoning affected only wild wood pigeons, while other species of birds and domestic animals did not show any symptoms.

Registered breeding of carrier pigeons, whose birds are compulsorily vaccinated and kept in a controlled environment, also remained without losses.

This also strengthened the conclusion that it was a locally planted toxic substance in the field.

The case is taken over by the police
After the poisoning was confirmed, the whole matter passes into the hands of the Police of the Czech Republic.

The municipal police announced that they would file a criminal complaint against an unknown perpetrator on suspicion of animal cruelty and unauthorized handling of dangerous substances.

The investigation is likely to be complicated – the poison was deployed in such a way that there are no direct witness statements or obvious clues leading to the perpetrator.

At the same time, the city and the police officers appreciated the cooperation of the public, who actively reported the findings of dead birds during March.

Thanks to this, it was possible to clean the affected sites quickly and minimize the risk of further poisoning, whether in animals or humans.

What happened in Most in the spring
From mid-March, dead pigeons began to appear in Most in increasing numbers.

Initially, these were individual finds, but over the course of several days, the officers collected dozens of pieces a day.

Technical services were on alert, the city issued warnings not to touch the birds, feed them and walk dogs on a leash.

The situation lasted approximately two months, during which diseases were gradually ruled out and the results of laboratory analyses were awaited.

It is now clear about the cause, but not who is responsible for the poisoning.

The search for the perpetrator continues.

People report cases of dead pigeons in the city, technical services are clearing them away.

# In Alcalá de Henares, storks were left without food and nids due to the decisions of the authorities

In Alcalá de Henares, storks were left without food and nids due to the decisions of the authorities

In Alcalá de Henares, the situation with the stork population is critical: the city's birds were left without their main source of food after the contract for the maintenance of the municipal feeding point expired almost a month ago.

According to local ecologists, for more than 20 days, storks have been forced to look for food in nature, which has dramatically complicated their survival during the breeding season.

Problems for storks began back in December, when an outbreak of bird flu in the region led to the death of almost a third of the recorded pairs.

In the spring, one of the oldest couples lost its nest on the tower of the city clock: by decision of the municipality, the structure was dismantled due to the threat of collapse, despite the fact that there were eggs in the nest.

Now food shortages have added to these difficulties - the city did not renew the contract for the supply of feed, and the birds were left without support in the most vulnerable period.

After the landfill was closed at the end of 2019, a special feeding point was created for them: up to 40 kilograms of frozen chickens were delivered there daily.

However, with the end of the contract, this measure stopped, and the birds again became dependent on scarce natural resources.

The question of the fate of the feeding point became the main one at the last meeting of the city council.

Representatives of the opposition suggested that the municipality should take over the funding if the inter-municipal association could not ensure the continuation of the work.

The authorities of Alcalá de Henares insist that the responsibility lies with the Mancomunitada of the East, which is now headed by the mayor of Loches.

Despite the approval of temporary funding in the amount of 5 thousand euros for three months, feeding has not yet been resumed.

Environmentalists warn: if food is not restored in the coming days, dozens of chicks will be at risk, which could lead to a further decline in the population.

According to their estimates, there are now about 80 young storks left in the city, and their survival directly depends on the prompt actions of the authorities.

After the dismantling of the nest at the city hall, storks tried to restore it to its original place, but to no avail.

Similar cases have happened before: birds have returned and built new nests even on obstacles set up to scare them away.

The municipality explains the dismantling by the need to ensure the safety of people and buildings, referring to expert opinions and coordination with regional environmental authorities.

The eggs from the nest were transferred to a specialized center for further breeding and subsequent return of the chicks to nature.

Alcalá de Henares is historically known as the city of storks: these birds have settled here since the XVII century, and some nests are considered older than many city buildings.

However, the reduction of the food base and interference with nesting have already led to a noticeable decrease in numbers.

Similar problems are recorded in other regions of Spain, where storks are gradually disappearing from cities.

For reference: storks in Spain traditionally settle on the roofs of churches and historical buildings, and their presence is considered a symbol of good luck and prosperity.

In recent years, the number of these birds in the country has been declining due to urbanization, reduction of natural foraging areas and changes in the waste management system.

Interference with nesting is regulated by regional and national laws, and any work with nests requires coordination with environmental authorities.

# Antimicrobial active substances excluded from veterinary use

Antimicrobial active substances excluded from veterinary use
Friday, 22 May, 2026
An important element in the fight against antimicrobial resistance is the promotion of a more prudent use of antimicrobials, which is also a priority in veterinary medicine.

In order to ensure the responsible use of antimicrobials, especially antibiotics, and to reduce the risk of resistance, the provisions of the EU and national legislation governing veterinary medicinal products must be complied with when providing veterinary services.

Based on the "One Health" approach, the relevant EU and national legislation and measures give priority to the protection and preservation of the effectiveness of active substances that are critically important from a human health point of view.

In line with this,
on the definition of antimicrobials or groups of antimicrobials reserved for the treatment of certain human infections in accordance with Regulation (EU) 2019/6 of the European Parliament and of the Council
Regulation (EU) No 2022/1255
contains a list of antimicrobial active substances that must not be used in veterinary medicines, medicated feed or for other veterinary purposes, including off-label use.

As a result of the evaluation carried out by the European Medicines Agency on the basis of the criteria set out in Regulation (EU) 2021/1760

The veterinary use of 18 groups of antibiotics, 18 antiviral agents and one antiprotozoal active ingredient is not allowed from 9 February 2023.

The use of these active substances and groups of active substances in animal health was not typical in Hungary even before this regulation.

At the same time, in order to comply with the law, it must be ensured that the human medicinal products concerned will not be prescribed on veterinary prescription in the future.

The relevant information was sent to the public pharmacies distributing medicinal products containing the given active ingredients through the National Centre for Public Health and Pharmacy in order to ensure that the human medicinal products concerned are not dispensed for veterinary purposes or on veterinary prescription.

At the same time, domestic food chain actors, including veterinary professionals and animal keepers, must pay special attention to the fact that these products are not prescribed and used.

The protection of these active ingredients, the reduction of the risk of resistance, and the preservation of the sensitivity of pathogens serve to protect the health of all of us!
